name: Liber Linteus
description: The Liber Linteus Zagrabiensis (Linen Book of Zagreb) is the longest extant text in the Etruscan language and the only surviving linen book from antiquity. It consists of linen wrappings used to mummify a woman in Ptolemaic Egypt, upon which Etruscan text had been painted. The text is believed to be a ritual calendar, with detailed instructions for religious ceremonies and offerings to various deities. While the basic function as a ritual calendar is generally accepted, the precise meaning of many passages remains obscure due to the limited understanding of the Etruscan language. Some alternative interpretations suggest the text contains hidden knowledge or prophecies, but these lack substantial evidence and are largely based on speculation. Mainstream scholarship focuses on deciphering the text through comparative linguistics and archaeological context, meticulously reconstructing the Etruscan religion and society based on the available linguistic and material evidence. The discovery of the Liber Linteus provided invaluable insight into Etruscan religious practices, shedding light on their complex system of gods, rituals, and beliefs. The text's unique preservation as mummy wrappings underscores the enduring appeal of Etruscan artifacts, even when repurposed in foreign lands.
subtitle: Etruscan Ritual Calendar on Linen Wrappings

claims.claim: The Liber Linteus contains precise instructions for complex religious rituals.
claims.evidence: Repetitive phrases, names of deities, and designated dates suggest a ritualistic function.
claims.criticism: While ritualistic intent is agreed upon, the exact nature of these rituals remains largely unknown due to the language barrier and lack of parallel texts.

claims.claim: The text holds encrypted prophecies related to the fate of the Etruscan civilization.
claims.evidence: Some proponents interpret certain passages as veiled predictions of future events, citing perceived correlations with historical occurrences.
claims.criticism: This interpretation lacks linguistic and historical rigor and is based on subjective readings rather than objective analysis. Mainstream scholarship rejects this as speculative.

claims.claim: The Liber Linteus reveals a deeper, more esoteric understanding of the cosmos among the Etruscans.
claims.evidence: The names of certain deities and recurring symbols are linked to astronomical phenomena and philosophical concepts.
claims.criticism: While the Etruscans certainly possessed astronomical knowledge and philosophical ideas, the Liber Linteus primarily focuses on practical religious practices. Attributing a fully developed esoteric system based solely on this text is an overreach.

keyFigures.name: Jacob Wisse
keyFigures.role: Early translator and researcher of the text.

keyFigures.name: Albert van der Meer
keyFigures.role: Leading Etruscan linguist who has contributed significantly to its interpretation.

keyFigures.name: Lammert Bouke van der Meer
keyFigures.role: Leading Etruscan archaeologist and linguist, specializing in Etruscan religion.

timeline.event: Estimated period of origin for the Liber Linteus text.
The linen book is taken to Egypt and used as mummy wrappings.
The mummy and its wrappings are acquired by Mihajlo Barić, a Croatian official.
The Liber Linteus is donated to the National Museum in Zagreb, Croatia.
Initial attempts at decipherment and interpretation begin.

sources.author: van der Meer, Lammert Bouke
sources.work: The Bronze Liver of Piacenza. Analysis of a Polytheistic Structure
sources.title: van der Meer, Lammert Bouke
